segments.text: So, today we are going to have lecture eight. So, let me first recall those things that
we have already done. We had learned about how to do algebra matrices, adding matrices,
subtracting matrices, but the main thing that we had focused last time was to take
the product of two matrices. And we had learned a bunch of ways of doing it. One of them
was taking the i-th row of matrix A and taking its dot product with the j-th column of matrix B.
Then we had also said that this thing could also be thought of as linear combination of columns of
A. So, linear combination of columns of A. The matrix product can also be thought of as linear
combination of rows of B. So, we had seen examples of all these things and as we go
along we will see how to keep on using this and which form is more advantageous in certain
regards compared to the others. Now, one thing I would like to remind you about the matrix
multiplication is matrix multiplication is a very special thing. And we have to keep in
mind that there are certain restrictions under which this matrix can be two matrices
can be multiplied. So, first restriction laws that if I have a matrix A of size m by n and a
matrix B of size n by B, the number of rows of sorry number of columns of A must match the
number of rows of B for the matrix multiplication A times B to be defined. So, if this condition
holds true then the resulting matrix would be of size m by p m rows and p columns and in which
case we can talk about the product of the two matrices. Now, the moment we see this,
this thing comes to us immediately that B times A need not be even defined. So,
the thing is this is first kind of non-intuitive thing that we see for the first time that A
times B need not be equal to B times A. So, the matrix multiplication is not commutative.
Now, this is the first time perhaps many people see things with an operation where things are
not commutative even though you would have seen division which is also not a commutative
operation, but this is another instance where you see matrix multiplication is not commutative.
And in fact, one can ask a question well under what circumstances A B will not be equal to B A.
For example, in this case we are saying A times B is defined because of the sizes of the A's and
B and B times A need not be even defined. For example, when we have A as a 3 by 4 matrix
and B as a 4 by 5 matrix, we know that A B would be of size 3 by 5, but B times A is not defined.
So, one can ask a question could it be possible that when both A B and B A are defined at
least in terms of sizes in that scenario can A B be equal to B A. So, let us see what if
A was of size 3 by 2 and B is of size 2 by 3. In this case both A B and B A are defined,
A B will be of size 3 by 3 and B times A would be of size 2 by 2. So, both of them are defined,
but as you can see because of the difference in the size A B will not be equal to B times A.
So, in some sense commutativity is not lost just because B times A is not defined even if
A B A times A is defined it could still happen that the two matrices are not the same. So,
then one may ask the question what if both matrices A B and B A are of the same size,
can it be true that A times B will always be equal to B times A. So, let us try a
scenario say A is of size 2 by 2 B is also size 2 by 2 then both A B and B A are of size
2 by 2. So, at least this time there is no disparity in the sizes can it still happen
that A times B need not be the same as B times A. You will see pretty much if you
pick any random matrix unless you just happen to be picking a diagonal matrix and stuff like
that you can check A times B will not be equal to B times A. So, the chances of you picking
two matrices which commute will be fairly low and the best way to come see that this thing is not
equal to that other product is if you just perform the matrix multiplication out here the
first entry would be 2 and in here the first entry would be 21. So, definitely these two
matrices are not equal to each other. So, it is not because of the sizing compatibility
it is not because of the lack of being defined it is just that matrix multiplication will lose
on commutativity because of the way things are defined. In fact, the inspiration behind
matrix multiplication is that of function composition and as you very well know function
composition is not commutative and pretty much the same thing is happening in matrices also.
Now, let us look into other kind of surprising scenarios which happen in matrix
multiplication things which you are kind of used to, but may no longer be true in matrix
multiplication. So, let us see what those things are these are some results which we kind of take
for granted when you are dealing with numbers, but they may no longer be true in matrices and
you have to be careful about them. So, let us try what are the things which we kind of
take for granted is things like this what would be a plus b whole square. We kind of have a habit
of writing it as a square plus 2 a b plus b square, but let us see how it will look like
in terms of matrix multiplication. So, a plus b times a plus b. So, we perform the matrix
multiplication we are assuming that a and b are of nice size. So, that all these multiplications
can be performed. So, without loss of generality let us assume that a and b are both square matrices.
So, that we do not have to worry about whether some multiplication is defined or not. So,
something like this plus b times b. Now, matrix multiplication is distributive. So,
using that idea we can get something like this. This is what we call as a square,
but out here we have to be careful we simply cannot say that this is this thing is 2 times
b a this need not be 2 times a times b or 2 times b times a because a times b need not be
equal to b times a. So, in general you cannot say anything except that over it will be a b
this is one of the things which we kind of love to do, but you have to be careful you
cannot simply write this as twice a b. Likewise we have seen that we have a
habit of writing things like this a minus b times a plus b these kind of things need
not hold for matrices. So, we have to be careful about these kind of identities we
will be tempted to use them many times, but we have to be we have to make sure whether they
will hold true or not. The another thing that is in my opinion even more surprising at least
when you see for the first time or at least it kind of tells you that you have to be even
more careful what if the product of 2 matrices is 0 does a v equal to 0 imply that a is 0.
This is the property that we use in real numbers or integers that product of 2 things is 0 implies
one of them is 0. It is a very fundamental property of things like real numbers complex
numbers integers because they are all what we call as an integral domain,
but is it true for matrices. So, let us take a simple example. So,
here is my matrix a this is 1 0 0 0 and here is my matrix b let us say 0 2 0 0. If you take the
product of these 2 matrices using any method that we have seen then you will get if you
multiply these 2 matrices 1 0 times 0 0 gives you 0 1 0 times 0 2 gives you 0 and you have
so a times b is 0 whereas, neither a nor b is 0. So, 2 non-zero matrices can combine to give
you a 0 matrix. So, we cannot say for sure that this is true this is not a 0 matrix this is
not a 0 matrix and yet we have a product of 0 matrix 0 matrix. So, keep in mind these kind
of anomalies that are there and that should prevent you from just taking this for granted.
So, let us see how this matrix multiplication helps us in doing what we want to do. See one
of the reasons we are interested in matrix multiplication is that not only it gives
me way to do algebra on matrices it also helps use matrices for many purposes. So,
one of the things we want to take care of is let me get this out of our way so that.
So, imagine up until now we have been looking at these system of equations we used to create
an augmented matrix out of it which was something like this. We used to do row
operations to talk about the solutions of this system, but with this new found knowledge of
matrix multiplication we can also write this as look at the coefficient matrix that we have here.
This is my coefficient matrix. So, this is 1 3 2 minus 3. I can rewrite the system of
equation as my coefficient matrix times the vector x y is equal to the right hand side
vector 5 7. So, from now on we can have the luxury of converting a system of equation
using matrix multiplication we can convert it into this form a x equal to b. The reason this
form is so convenient for us is because it reminds us of the way we deal with the equation.
So, for example we all grew up with looking at equations like this 3 x is equal to 2 kind
of reminds us you of that. How do you solve this? Well you say oh I am going to multiply
by the inverse of 3 on both the sides which is one third and this is how you solve. So,
we are hoping that maybe something like this can be done for matrices somehow we can talk
about inverse of a matrix and try to get the vector x using the idea of inverses.
Can it be done or how will it be done? These are questions we are going to answer in future,
but this is the reason why we are so fond of matrix multiplication because it kind of
us think of system of equations exactly like the way we are used to. Now before we exploit
matrix multiplication there are few things which I want to make sure we understand.
It is about some special kind of matrices since we are going to use those kind of
matrices every now and then. So, it is a good idea to at least be aware of their
definitions. Now all the definitions that I am going to discuss now would be for square matrices.
So, all these special things that we have or special matrices are all square matrices. So,
we can think of them as n by n matrices. So, the first matrix that we want to talk about
is the so called diagonal matrix. So, what is a diagonal matrix? Well as the name suggests
it is a square matrix which has entries only on the diagonal. So, that means we will have things
like some bunch of entries on the diagonals everything of the diagonal is just a 0 matrix.
So, what we are saying is all the entries a i j are 0 if i is not equal to j. Notice we are
saying anything about the entries when i is equal to j. That means those entries could be 0,
they could not be 0, but all we care about is things above the diagonal and things below
the diagonal should be 0. Any such matrix we will call it as a diagonal matrix.
Then there is an upper triangular matrix as the name suggests the shape of this matrix would
be. So, imagine if you have a square matrix you can split it into two halves along the
diagonal. So, if we have a matrix where the lower half is all 0 and the upper half could have
0 or non-zero entries then we will say we have a upper triangular matrix. So, in terms of this
we can say all the entries which are in the lower half of the matrix are 0. So,
you want to describe the lower half you will say a i j is 0 wherever i is bigger than j.
If that is true then we say that we have a lower sorry upper triangular matrix. Likewise
we have the lower triangular matrix where as you can guess we are saying that the lower end
of the matrix could be anything, but the upper end of the matrix above the diagonal should all
be 0. So, in this case we will say that a i j is 0 wherever i is less than j. So,
these are some special matrices they will have their own usefulness as we go along.
There is one more matrix that we would like to talk about this is the so called identity
matrix. This matrix has very nice property we generally refer it to as i sub n and it has
ones along the main diagonal and 0 everywhere else. So, it is a diagonal matrix with ones
as the diagonal entries and the beauty of this matrix is if I have any n by n matrix
and I multiply it by this identity matrix it will give back my matrix A.
So, in a manner of speaking the matrix i n or the identity matrix acts like one. It is the same
kind of thing that one does in real numbers you multiply one by any number you will get
back the same number. So, in general in mathematics whenever we see such a notion
if you multiply by this special object any object of your set and if you get back your object
then that special object is acting like the multiplicative identity.
Let us see what else do we want oh yeah there is one more special matrix useful guy remember
all these are definitions called square matrices. So, the concept of diagonal
upper triangular lower triangular we are confining them to square matrices.
So, another one which we are interested in is the so called permutation matrix. So,
the permutation matrix is basically a matrix obtained by permuting the rows of the identity
matrix. So, for example if you look at a 3 by 3 identity matrix and suppose I permute its
rows say something like this. This is our example of a permutation matrix because this
has been known this is our example of a permutation matrix because we have obtained this matrix by
permuting the rows of identity matrix. Now, why are we so fond of this matrix?
You will see permutation matrix exactly does what the name suggests it permutes things.
So, for example look at what will happen if I take x y z and multiply it using a
permutation matrix. I will my upon matrix multiplication this is what I am going to get
z x y. So, the effect of a permutation matrix on a vector is basically permuting that vector
according to what the permutation was for your permutation matrix.
This is the thing which will help us in row swapping. So, this is the matrix which is
helpful for row swaps. Now, that we that we have seen a few of the special matrices.
Let us try to see what else is there which can be kind of useful for us.
Ok. In fact, if you look at all possible permutation matrices of size 3 suppose I create
a set of all matrices of size 3 such that A is a permutation matrix. You can see that this set
is closed under multiplication. What it means is if I take a matrix A and I take a matrix B both
of them are permutation matrices then the product of those two permutation matrices will
still be a permutation matrix. So, in fact, if any of you knows things in group theory
this is basically saying that the set of permutation matrices is forming a group under
matrix multiplication. By the way in this particular case let me call this as S3. So,
that I am reminded that it is a 3 by 3 matrix. If you think about it the number of elements
in this matrix oh sorry this set would be 3 factorial because you have 3 rows to permute
you can do them in 3 factorial ways. So, this set will have size 6. So, those of us who may
know group theory you can think of it as group of size 6. Ok. Anyway this is a little bit of
a digression but let us see what else we are after any problems the marker in here.
Ok now let me introduce an operational matrices which is kind of very unique to matrices.
We do not see it in other contexts but it is kind of a useful thing to know what matrices
and this operation is that of a transpose. The notation we have for this is A with us
a exponent kind of known for of t it is not actually an exponent it is just a symbol as a
superscript you can say. So, suppose I have a matrix A which is of size m by n notice we
are no longer confining ourselves to square matrices. So, if I have A which is of size m
by n then we define the transpose of A as a matrix of size n by m. So, basically the rows
of A become the columns of A transpose. So, this is how we obtain A transpose and vice versa. So,
for example if I have a matrix A which say looks like 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 then the transpose of A
will be the matrix where rows of A will become the column of A transpose 1, 2, 3,
4, 5, 6. So, as you can see we started with a 2 by 3 matrix and A transpose happens to be a
3 by 2 matrix. Now, one may wonder why are we so fond of just flipping it. The thing is
transpose operation has quite usefulness for us which will not be evident for some time.
So, let us at least just play with the transpose operation and see what all things
it can do for us. So, there are some properties of the transpose which are easy to verify
which I leave it for you as an exercise. So, one of the properties of the transpose is
if you take the sum of two matrices and its transpose it is basically the sum of the transposes.
If you take a scalar multiple of a matrix and its transpose then the scalar multiple
as expected will not change and it will be k times A transpose. Perhaps the most
useful of the properties of transposes is this one which will take a slight bit more
effort to prove this is AB transpose is basically B transpose E transpose.
Notice how the order has changed and if you think a little bit it will be kind of
intuitively clear why this order should change. So, for example, if A was a M by N matrix,
B was a N by P matrix, in that case A transpose must be a N by N matrix,
B transpose must be a P by N matrix. So, you can pretty much guess from the sizes of the A
and the B transposes that the only multiplication that could possibly be defined in a general
scenario would be A transpose times A transpose because then only these two guys are going to
match. So, in some sense it is an intuitive way to guess the validity of the formula,
but the last this property is something which I highly recommend you to try to write a proof.
It is not a very difficult proof or at the same time not a very easy one also and it gives
you a good practice in writing a general proof regarding matrix multiplication.
So, let us see some nice things about transpose some of the properties it can help us with.
So, one of the things the transpose helps us in is defining properties about matrices.
There are two types of matrices symmetric matrices and skew symmetric. So, we say a
matrix is symmetric if that matrix is the same as its transpose that means the operation
of transpose does not change the matrix if that happens then it is a symmetric matrix
and we have another type called the skew symmetric matrix. So, we will say A is skew symmetric
A transpose happens to be minus of notice just by the definition itself it is quite evident
that the idea of a symmetric or a skew symmetric matrix can only be implemented on a square matrix
because you want this thing to be a multiple of its transpose. So, the sizes must of that A
transpose and A must be the same which can only happen for a square matrix. So, here is
something worth noticing consider this matrix A time A plus A transpose.
Suppose, I have a matrix A which is a square matrix. So, A transpose must also be a square
matrix the same size. So, that means, we can talk about A plus A transpose let me call this
matrix as B is B symmetric suppose I want to test whether B will be symmetric or not.
You see to test symmetry all I need to do is talk about what is B transpose.
Well since B is A plus A transpose. So, B transpose would be the transpose of this
then by using property number one the transpose of this would be A transpose plus A transpose
transpose question is what would be A transpose transpose you think about it in the definition
of A transpose A transpose will flip the rows to columns, but taking another transpose will
flip it back to rows. So, rows becomes column then column become back to rows.
So, that means, A transpose transpose must be A itself. So, this is another property that we
could have added to our list that the transpose of a transpose give you back A. So, in some
sense transpose is an operation which applied twice brings you back to A these kind of
operations have a name they usually called the idempotent operations,
but do not worry about the name they are not going to let me help you or hurt you here.
A transpose plus A is same as B. So, since B transpose turns out to be equal to B
therefore, we can say B is symmetric. So, if you take any matrix A add to its transpose
then the sum matrix will always be a symmetric matrix. Let us try with one more thing let us
read another matrix C which is of the form A minus A transpose is C symmetric or skew
symmetric. So, let us try it again and see what happens to C transpose C transpose will be
A minus A transpose whole transpose transpose can be distributed in a manner of speaking
one of the properties we saw. So, this becomes A transpose minus A.
Now, look at this the relationship between A transpose minus A and A minus A transpose
would be simply by multiplying it by the scale and minus 1. So, now, we have C transpose
as minus C therefore, we can say that C is skew symmetric. So, at this stage one may wonder
if I have if I am given a matrix in general is it always the case that either it will be
symmetric or skew symmetric or could there be matrices which are neither symmetric nor
skew symmetric. So, let us try to answer that and see where does that take us.
For example, let us take a look at this matrix 1 2 3 4. Suppose this is A and A
transpose would be of the same size 1 2 3 4. As you can see A transpose is neither equal to
A nor it is equal to minus A. Therefore, neither this matrix is symmetric nor it is
skew symmetric. So, in fact, being a symmetric matrix or being a skew symmetric matrix is
kind of a special thing for a matrix and once it has that property then it can help us
understand its behavior of it better. Let us make one more quick observation.
Suppose I look at a 2 by 2 matrix let me call it as ABCD. I want to know do the entries
have to be something special for this to be a skew symmetric matrix. So, that means,
if I look at A transpose this will be ABCD. If I want anti-symmetry or sorry skew symmetry
for skew symmetric I want A transpose to be equal to minus A which means that the matrix
that I have written for my A transpose must be equal to the matrix I get by multiplying
each of these guys by a minus. Now remember these two matrices must be the same. So,
A must be the same as minus A, D must be the same as minus D which can only happen
when A and D are 0. So, what does this tell us? This tells us that for a skew
matrix the diagonal entries must be 0 even though I have shown you only on a 2 by 2 case
but for a general case the same idea will work and you can try to generalize it for any n by n
matrix. So, here is a result which we can keep for us. It is again a result which should help
us in future. If A is a skew symmetric matrix the diagonal entries have to be 0.
Okay. Okay. I should kind of caution at this stage about one thing not that it will affect
to most of us at this stage or at this level but this result that I have written
is true pretty much for almost every kind of situation except when you are working
in very special field. For example, if you are working in field mod 2 then A could be equal
minus A without A being 0. So, having said that I know that the statement may not make much sense
for many of us but these kind of things you should always keep in mind that they are being
done in very special fields like real numbers and all that life is quite comfortable and not
very surprising. Anyways, this is something that that will have some impact later on.
Here is a question for us. If you recall given any function f of x you can always write it as
a sum of an even function plus an odd function. Suppose my function is defined over all real
nine then I can always write it as an even function plus an odd function. This is
something that sometimes you see in calculus. We ask the same question over here given any
matrix A. Of course, we are talking about a square matrix A. Is it possible for us to write
it as the sum of a symmetric matrix plus a skew symmetric matrix? Can every A be written as
a symmetric matrix and a skew symmetric matrix? The answer turns out to be kind of surprisingly
simple. The idea these two things are actually very very similar to each other in construction
and that is the beauty of the math behind it. So, if I have a matrix A a square matrix I want
to write it as the sum of a symmetric matrix let me call that to be B and a skew symmetric
matrix let me call that to be C. So, I want B to be symmetric and I want C to be skew
symmetric. So, let us see what is A transpose. A transpose would be B transpose plus C transpose
but didn't we say that B is symmetric. So, B transpose must be B and C transpose would
be minus C because C is anti-symmetric. Now, I can solve for B and C. If I add these two
matrix equations I will get B must be A plus A transpose over 2 and if I subtract these two
I will get C as A minus A transpose over. If you remember just a while ago we have already
shown that A plus A transpose is a symmetric matrix A minus A transpose is a skew symmetric
matrix. So, we can always write any matrix as a sum of a symmetric plus a skew symmetric
matrix. So, one more question what is the advantage of doing it? Well one thing is it is
skew symmetric matrix. That means, if we have some idea about how symmetric matrices behave
perhaps that can help us understand how my matrix will behave. So, we will take we will
continue from here in the next session.
